headline: Self taught, full stack Marketer, creator and maker.

From being a teenager making full-time income from blogging in 2007 to helping businesses generate MQLs worth well over  US$10 M+ in 2018, I have been fortunate to have a journey of constant learning and upskilling in digital projects. 

Out of running blogs and providing SEO services as a freelancer, in 2009 I co-founded Kerala’s first specialized digital marketing agency delivering services to businesses in India, Middle, East, Canada and the UK as COO.

In 2014, I deviated from the startup path to join Fingent, a New York headquartered custom software development company to lead the digital marketing line until 2018. I was responsible for team that consulted and executed digital projects for both internal and external clients.

headline: Director of Demand Generation | B2B Growth Marketing Leader | UX Evangelist
about:
Digital marketing is a fickle space. It's a world full of tools and technologies, best practices and TLAs (three letter acronyms). In some organizations, it's a revenue driver. While in others, it's responsible for operations and reporting. With more business fully embracing the digital era of marketing, we are far from a "standard" setup for digital marketing. However, no matter what your responsibilities are, there is one thing that every digital marketer must keep in mind: the user experience of the individual you're marketing to.

With all of the tracking cookies and live chat tools at your disposal, it's easy to forget that there's another person on the other side of the monitor. As MarTech stacks grow, I see marketers losing sight of one of the most fundamental pieces of good marketing. You are there to help that person find what they are looking for as quickly as possible. That's it. Sure, it would be great to collect some more information on that form or get a few more leads by gating that piece of content. But as soon as you lose the UX mindset and start putting your marketing priorities above the experience of the visitor, that's when bounce rates start to rise and the flow of leads slows down. 

This is why I will continue to preach to anyone who'll listen that you always need to keep the person you are marketing to in mind. From the campaign all the way down to the individual programs and tactics. Try your own stuff. Test your landing pages. I think what the marketing team at Drift has been doing recently is a perfect example of this done at a very high level.

We all have goals to hit and KPIs to track. But I can speak from experience when I assure you that if you build your marketing strategy by optimizing for the user experience, you'll be surprised how quickly you start getting the results you're looking for.

Don't get caught up in the MarTech black hole. 

Focus on the marketing user experience. 

(You can call it MUX if you need a new TLA.)
